title: Intramuscular and Subcutaneous Injection Technique
subtitle: The nurse gives IM and subcutaneous injections safely — correct site, angle, needle, and volume, the Z-track method for irritating IM meds, no aspiration/massage for subQ heparin, and safe sharps disposal.

patient.name: Injection Technique
patient.admitDate: The nurse administers intramuscular and subcutaneous injections using the correct site, angle, needle, volume, and sharps safety.

nursesNote: The nurse administers intramuscular and subcutaneous injections using correct technique. For IM injections the ventrogluteal site is preferred (away from major nerves and vessels) and the dorsogluteal site is avoided due to sciatic nerve risk; the deltoid is used for small volumes and vaccines and the vastus lateralis for infants. IM injections are given at a 90-degree angle, and the Z-track method (pull the skin laterally, inject, wait about 10 seconds, withdraw, then release without massaging) is used for irritating medications to prevent leakage and tissue staining. Subcutaneous injections are given at a 45-90 degree angle with a short, small-gauge needle into the abdomen, upper arm, or thigh, avoiding the area around the umbilicus and rotating sites. Subcutaneous heparin is not aspirated or massaged. The nurse lets the antiseptic dry before injecting, never recaps a used needle, and disposes of all sharps in the proper container.
providerOrders: Use the ventrogluteal site for IM injections; avoid the dorsogluteal site
Give IM injections at 90 degrees and subcutaneous at 45-90 degrees
Use the Z-track method for irritating IM medications
Do not aspirate or massage after subcutaneous heparin; rotate sites
Never recap a used needle; dispose of sharps in the proper container

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about injection technique?

opts.text: The ventrogluteal site is preferred for IM injections
IM injections are given at a 90-degree angle
Subcutaneous injections are given at a 45-90 degree angle
The deltoid can safely hold a 3 mL IM injection
Subcutaneous heparin should not be aspirated or massaged
Injection sites should be rotated to prevent tissue damage
The Z-track method keeps medication from leaking into subcutaneous tissue
The dorsogluteal site is avoided because of sciatic nerve risk
A larger volume can go in the ventrogluteal site than the deltoid
Aspiration is required before every vaccine
Subcutaneous abdominal injections avoid the area around the umbilicus
A smaller, higher-gauge needle is used for subcutaneous injections

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order the steps to give an IM injection

opts.text: Verify the order and rights and perform hand hygiene
Select and locate the correct site (e.g., ventrogluteal)
Clean the site and let the antiseptic dry
Insert at 90 degrees and inject the medication
Withdraw, apply gentle pressure, and dispose of the needle
Using the ventrogluteal site for an IM injection
Choosing the dorsogluteal site for routine IM injections
Inserting an IM needle at a 90-degree angle
Massaging the site after subcutaneous heparin
Giving a 3 mL injection into the deltoid
Aspirating before a subcutaneous insulin injection
Rotating injection sites
Using Z-track for an irritating IM medication

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: Order the Z-track steps for an irritating IM med
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Position the patient and locate the ventrogluteal site
Pull the skin and subcutaneous tissue laterally
Insert at 90 degrees and inject slowly
Wait about 10 seconds, then withdraw the needle
Release the skin and do not massage the site
Selecting the ventrogluteal site for an IM injection
Injecting 3 mL into the deltoid muscle
Using Z-track for an irritating IM medication
Massaging the site after subcutaneous heparin
Rotating subcutaneous insulin sites
Recapping a used needle by hand
Letting the antiseptic dry before injecting
Reusing the same needle for a second injection
